coredata.dc:title: B ← N coordination bond reinforced dynamic fluorescence of pH-responsive amphiphile alginate aggregates for anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Riting
He, Furui
Yang, Shujuan
Mai, Keyang
Huang, Shuntian
Liu, Haifang
Feng, Yuhong
Yu, Gaobo
Li, Jiacheng
coredata.dc:description: High quantum yield polysaccharide-based materials are significative for the dynamic anti-counterfeiting, while that are limited by weak fluorescence. However, natural polysaccharides with weak fluorescence are not suitable for anti-counterfeiting. Herein, alginate derivatives (SA-PBA) exhibiting aggregation-induced emission with high-quantum yields were synthesized by grafting phenylboronic acid (PBA) onto a sodium alginate (SA) chain. As the concentration increases, polymer assembly can be induced to form more compact soft colloidal aggregates, which enhances the fluorescence properties of alginate derivatives by introducing B ← N coordination bonds in the hydrophobic microregions. Interestingly, the clustered aggregates of SA-PBA can be dynamically controlled by pH, realizing the reversible adjustment of fluorescence. The corresponding mechanism is revealed by the combination of coarse-grained simulations and experiments. It is found that SA-PBA uses a hydrophobic driving force and hydrogen bond interaction to self-assemble in an aqueous solution and promote fluorescence emission. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield of SA-PBA can reach 14.4 % and can be reversibly altered by tuning soft colloidal microstructures. Therefore, a reversible information encryption system of SA-PBA is developed for anti-counterfeiting. This work shed some light on how to design novel anti-counterfeit materials based on natural polysaccharides and optimize the dynamic fluorescence conditions.

TEM imagine of SA-PBA at the concentration (d) 0.5 mg/ml, (e) 2 mg/ml. (f) Particle size distribution of SA-PBA colloids at various concentrations.

The fluorescent characterization for the SA-PBA: (a) The fluorescent spectrum. (b) Fluorescence spectra at different concentrations, the excitation wavelength was 345 nm. (c) Lifetime and quantum yield of SA-PBA in solid and liquid state. (d) Comparison of quantum yields of 2-APBA,3-APBA, SA-PBA, and SA. (e) The relationship of the fluorescence intensity and the logarithm of SA-PBA concentration in water, the inset shows the fluorescence photograph of different concentrations of SA-PBA under 365 nm UV light. (f) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the sample and other radiation times.

Reversible polymerization of SA-PBA by regulating pH: (a) The fluorescence spectra of SA-PBA at different pH values, the excitation wavelength was 345 nm. (b) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity and different pH values. Inset: Fluorescence photograph of varying pH values(3, 7, and 9) under 365 nm UV light. (c) Acid/alkali-controlled reversible fluorescence change of SA-PBA. (d)) Hydrodynamic size of SA-PBA when sequentially adding NaOH solution (2 M) and HCl solution (2 M) for five cycles.

head.ce:title: B ← N coordination bond reinforced dynamic fluorescence of pH-responsive amphiphile alginate aggregates for anti-counterfeiting

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High quantum yield polysaccharide-based materials are significative for the dynamic anti-counterfeiting, while that are limited by weak fluorescence. However, natural polysaccharides with weak fluorescence are not suitable for anti-counterfeiting. Herein, alginate derivatives (SA-PBA) exhibiting aggregation-induced emission with high-quantum yields were synthesized by grafting phenylboronic acid (PBA) onto a sodium alginate (SA) chain. As the concentration increases, polymer assembly can be induced to form more compact soft colloidal aggregates, which enhances the fluorescence properties of alginate derivatives by introducing B ← N coordination bonds in the hydrophobic microregions. Interestingly, the clustered aggregates of SA-PBA can be dynamically controlled by pH, realizing the reversible adjustment of fluorescence. The corresponding mechanism is revealed by the combination of coarse-grained simulations and experiments. It is found that SA-PBA uses a hydrophobic driving force and hydrogen bond interaction to self-assemble in an aqueous solution and promote fluorescence emission. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield of SA-PBA can reach 14.4 % and can be reversibly altered by tuning soft colloidal microstructures. Therefore, a reversible information encryption system of SA-PBA is developed for anti-counterfeiting. This work shed some light on how to design novel anti-counterfeit materials based on natural polysaccharides and optimize the dynamic fluorescence conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Information security and anti-counterfeiting technologies are crucial for safeguarding consumers against the increasing prevalence of forge (Fan et al., 2020; Gou et al., 2022). Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) fluorescent materials emit strongly when solid or aggregated (Luo et al., 2001). Intelligent AIE light-emitting materials that dynamically change fluorescent colors in response to external stimuli have gained widespread recognition as ideal anti-counterfeiting materials (Dou et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021; Zuo et al., 2022). Therefore, there is an increasing demand for green and sustainable fluorescent materials (Gu et al., 2018), particularly AIEgen-modified polysaccharides, that exhibit a smart fluorescence color-shifting response to external stimuli that can be used for anti-authentication (Dou et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2018). For example, Polysaccharide hydrogels synthesized by incorporating a pH-responsive fluorescent molecule into chitosan exhibit shape memory and switchable fluorescence behavior from being colorless to becoming bright cyan (Jian et al., 2018). Light-stimulated cellulose modified using an epoxy-functionalized latex containing a spiropyran moiety exhibits reversible photochromic behavior from being colorless to becoming purple (Abdollahi et al., 2016). Therefore, stimuli-responsive polysaccharide-based materials that exhibit dynamic fluorescence present would be an advanced technology for anti-counterfeiting.

Being a natural polysaccharide, sodium alginate (SA) is composed of mannuronic (M) and guluronic acids (G) (Gao et al., 2017). The G block is stiffer and more extended than the M block due to its higher degree of hindered rotation around the glycosidic linkages. Therefore, SA can provide a rigid microenvironment that ultimately reduces non-radiative relaxation pathways and enhances the fluorescent properties of the polymer due to its semi-rigid molecular chains compared with other natural polysaccharides (Dou et al., 2020). Pure SA-emitting materials have limited applications due to their low fluorescence quantum yield. Alginate-based AIE materials that show excellent biocompatibility and degradation qualities have been prepared using covalent or non-covalent approaches (Lee & Mooney, 2012; Lin, Liu, et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2011). For non-covalent modifications of SA, multiple hydrogen bonds are formed between the carboxyl group of gallic acid and the carboxyl or hydroxyl group of the SA chain, facilitating spin-orbit coupling and inducing the afterglow emission of gallic acid (Wan, Zeng, et al., 2022). The bright gray fluorescence of SA molecules is due to chain entanglement-induced oxygen and carboxyl group clustering and subsequent space electron cloud conjugation (Dou et al., 2018). Amphiphilic alginate-based fluorescent polymers synthesized by introducing a 4-(1,2, 2-triphenylvinyl) aniline group onto the SA skeleton exhibit solid green emission (Lin, Zhao, et al., 2021). Red-emitting fluorescent polymer nanoparticles have been synthesized by forming dynamic covalent bonds (Schiff base) between the amino group of Phe-NH2 and the aldehyde group of oxidized alginate (Wan et al., 2017). However, Alginate-based materials with strong fluorescence emission via AIE small molecules covalently grafted to the SA backbone are not favorable for anti-counterfeiting because they lack tunable fluorescence properties in response to external stimuli. Therefore, we hypothesize that the luminescence properties of alginate-based polymers are closely related to the self-assembly microstructure. Owing to the variable and controllable self-regulating microstructure, alginate-based soft colloids are likely to achieve different luminescence properties through aggregate behavior regulation. It is desirable to design alginate derivatives with responsive microstructure regulation to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield and controllability of luminescence in soft colloidal aggregates. Furthermore, stimuli-responsive alginate-based soft colloids are expected to become a novel anti-counterfeiting material.

Reversible non-covalent and covalent bonds have been widely applied in fluorescent materials exhibiting stimuli responses under environmental stress (Ao et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2021). Boron is capable of accepting an external lone pair of electrons to form a coordination complex with boron (Mellerup & Wang, 2019; Shi et al., 2018). The formation of a hydrogen bond between acylhydrazone and the hydroxyl group of butanol in a fluorescent material induces cleavage of the intramolecular B ← N coordination bond, which changes the planarity of the molecule and promotes a blue shift in the emission wavelength, ultimately changing the fluorescence emission color from orange-red to green (Sun et al., 2023). The fluorescence emission shifts from orange to green when highly Lewis acidic trinaphthylborane and pyridine form four-coordinated B ← N adducts due to the intermolecular B ← N coordination bonds altering the planarity of the molecular, causing the disruption of π-conjugation through the p orbital of boron (Matsuo et al., 2014). The conformational transition of the B ← N coordination bond can be induced using pH, thereby modulating the fluorescence properties of materials (Shimogawa et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2023). Based on this, if the colloid microstructure is tuned by introducing a B ← N coordination bond into the SA chain, the fluorescence properties of SA are enhanced. Therefore, we anticipate that the multidimensional design and programming of reconfigurable alginate architectonics via external stimulus-altered regulation can be achieved by incorporating the B ← N coordination bonds-engineered self-assembly system. This would further realize green, sustainable, luminescent biopolymeric materials with high quantum yield and controllability.

In our previous study, the size, Zeta potential, and morphology of various amphiphilic SA micelle was investigated through the DLS and TEM study at different pH values (Fang et al., 2020; Lin, Zhao, et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2018). The results show that the physicochemical property of the micelles formed by amphiphilic SA correlates with the degree of protonation of the carboxylic groups on the SA chain. Herein, amphiphilic alginate derivative (SA-PBA) with AIE properties was obtained after modification with 3-APBA via the Ugi reaction. SA-PBA is almost non-luminescent in diluted solutions but emits light upon aggregation. The B ← N coordination bond is a dynamic bond that can be reversibly adjusted by changes in pH value. The microscopic structure of amphiphilic SA-PBA soft colloidal aggregates was further investigated by controlling the protonation of carboxyl groups and the formation of B ← N coordination by changing the pH value, to understand the microstructure of soft colloidal aggregates on the luminescence properties of SA-PBA. The formation of the intramolecular B ← N coordination bond affects the flatness of the molecule, promoting the redshift of the SA-PBA emission peak and enhancing the fluorescence emission. Therefore, the emission intensity of SA-PBA exhibits reversible adjustment due to pH, which can control the assembly/disassembly of the B ← N coordination bonds. CGMD offers insights into the mesoscopic properties of macroscopic experimental systems because of the molecular complexity and microscopic heterogeneity of polysaccharides (Marchetto et al., 2020; Xu & Matysiak, 2017). CGMD demonstrates the aggregation behavior of hydrophobically alginate-based materials in aqueous solutions at different concentrations from the molecular scale. SA-PBA spontaneously forms colloid aggregates as increasing the concentration, resulting in fluorescence emission. This study is expected to provide design inspiration for developing novel stimulus-responsive smart polysaccharide materials.

Sodium alginate (SA, 200 ± 20 mPa·s, the G/M ratio determined via circular dichroism is 1.74, Mw = 332 kDa, Mn = 226 kDa), 3-Aminophenylboric acid hydrochloride (3-APBA, 98 %) and formaldehyde (AR) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The molecular weights of SA and SA-PBA were determined by GPC-MALLS using the methods of Andersen et al. with slight modifications (Andersen et al., 2012; Ulset et al., 2014), refer to SI for detail. Cyclohexyl isonitrile, Hydrochloric acid(HCl), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Macklin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

The synthesis of SA-PBA was a repeat of the previous work with slight modifications (Yan et al., 2016), following the steps of Scheme 1 . SA (6.00 g) was dissolved in pure water (300 mL) for the SA stock solution (2 wt%). The pH of the SA stock solution was adjusted to 3.6 with a 1 M HCl solution. Simultaneously, 3-APBA (0.33 g) and formaldehyde (0.075 mL) were dissolved in 20 mL of pure water and stirred at room temperature for three hours to form the reaction solution via a Schiff base reaction. Then, the reaction solution was added to the SA stock solution and stirred vigorously for 30 min. Following this, cyclohexyl isonitrile (0.33 mL) was added to the reaction solution and stirred at room temperature under dark conditions for 24 h. The crude reaction products were dialyzed with dialysis bags (3500 Da) in pure water for five days to remove any unreacted residue. The resulting product (SA-PBA) was then obtained by freeze-drying. As shown in Table S1 and Fig. S1a, GPC-MALLS was used to determine the molecular weight of the SA-PBA polymer (Mw = 276 kDa, Mn = 154 kDa). SA-PBA has a lower molecular weight than SA. The decrease in molecular weight may be attributed to the hydrolysis of the alginate backbone during the Ugi reaction (He et al., 2021; Timell, 1964).

1H NMR was conducted using a nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (AVANCE NEO 400, BRUKER Company, Germany), with SA-PBA dissolved in D2O at a concentration of 10 mg/ml. FTIR spectra of the samples were recorded using an FTIR converter (T27, BRUKER company, Germany) with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1. The spectra were scanned from 4000 to 500 cm−1. The solid was measured for UV–vis absorbance using the Lambda 750 s spectrophotometer (PE Company, USA).

The SA-PBA solution was applied to the ultra-thin carbon films and subsequently rinsed with a 2 % phosphotungstate solution. Prior to this, the solution was naturally air-dried at room temperature. The resultant morphology was observed using TEM (Talos F200X G2, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The SA-PBA solution at various pH conditions was applied to the copper mesh and subsequently rinsed with a 2 % phosphotungstate solution. Before this, the solution was dried under infrared light. The resultant morphology was observed with JEM-2100 high-resolution TEM (JEOL, Japan) under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The dynamic light scattering Zetasizer (DLS, Nano-ZS90, Malvern instrument, UK) was used to measure the hydrodynamic diameter of SA-PBA at various concentrations. Each sample was measured at least three times to ensure accuracy.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using an Axis Supra photoelectron spectroscopy instrument (Kratos, Japan). The charge effect was adjusted with C 1 s =284.8 eV in order to calibrate the binding energy scale. The findings were assessed with XPS peak 4.1.0.0 software (Chemistry, CUHK).

All fluorescence parameters for SA-PBA were recorded using an FLS 1000 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, U. K.). Emission spectra for different SA-PBA solutions were simultaneously measured under parallel conditions with an excitation wavelength of 345 nm. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using EPL with an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. Quantum yields (QY) were determined using a coated integrating sphere with an excitation wavelength of 345 nm. Each sample was measured three times.

The rheological properties of the SA-PBA solution were determined using a DHR rotational rheometer (Discovery HR-2, TA, USA) with a 60 mm stainless steel parallel plate geometry selected for Parr paste. The shear viscosity of solutions was evaluated by determining the apparent viscosity at shear rates ranging from 0.01 to 100 s−1.

Coarse-grained molecular dynamics (CGMD) was used to simulate the self-aggregation behavior of amphiphilic SA-PBA in aqueous solution. The GROMACS V2019.6 package with the MARTINI CG force field V2.2 was selected for all calculations (López et al., 2009; Marchetto et al., 2020). The equations of motion were solved utilizing the leap-frog algorithm with a time step of 20 fs. The Lennard-Jones (L-J) potential was calculated with a cutoff of 1.1 nm, and the coulomb potential was treated by a reaction field with a relative dielectric constant of 15. Constant-temperature (NVT) ensembles with periodic boundary conditions were used. Maintain the temperature at 300 K (tau_t = 8.0) using the nose-and-hoover thermostat (Evans & Holian, 1985). Energy minimization was performed for all simulations, followed by NVT pre-equilibration using a velocity-rescaled thermostat (Bussi et al., 2007). All systems reached equilibrium within 1 μs and were scalable up to 2 μs when simulating data from sample analysis. Each system was run at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of acid/alkali on the fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of SA-PBA was tested to determine its sensitivity to acid/alkali. Initially, the solution of SA-PBA at a concentration of 2 mg/ml was selected for testing, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to pH 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 using 2 M HCl and 2 M NaOH solutions. The resulting mixture was incubated for 5 min before measuring fluorescence intensity. For the pH-controlled reversible polymerization experiment of SA-PBA, 4 mL of SA-PBA solution (5 mg/ml) was mixed with 6 mL of pure water, then 10 μL of NaOH solution (2 M) and 10 μL of HCl solution (2 M) were added to the SA-PBA solution sequentially and circulated five times. The resulting mixture is used for the fluorescence intensity measurement. All fluorescence spectra were measured at a maximum excitation wavelength of 345 nm.

SA is a naturally occurring macromolecule with good hydrophilicity. SA can be modified by functionalizing the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on its molecular backbone to form alginate derivatives (Yadav et al., 2014). Herein, we synthesize alginate derivatives (SA-PBA) with stimulus-responsive and amphiphilic properties by grafting hydrophobic 3-APBA onto the SA chain via the Ugi reaction. The structure of SA-PBA was analyzed and determined using 1H NMR, FT-IR, and UV–Vis spectroscopies to confirm the presence of 3-APBA. As shown in Fig. S2a, the 1H NMR peaks observed at 3.5–4.0 ppm are attributed to the protons of the natural SA chain. Compared to the 1H NMR peaks of pure SA, new peaks are observed for synthetic SA-PBA duo to the phenyl ring in the 3-APBA moiety (7.3–7.9 ppm) (Wu et al., 2022) and cyclohexyl group (1.3–1.8 ppm) (Yan et al., 2016). The ratio between the integral of the 3-APBA and SA in the 1H NMR spectra was used to calculate the degree of substitution (DS) (Chiang & Chu, 2015). Based on the integral peak area (S1) of characteristic H-3 proton of APBA (= 7.3–7.9 ppm) and the integral peak area (S2) of alginate protons from = 3.5–4.0 ppm, DS of 3-APBA along the polymer was determined. The DS of 3-APBA grafted to SA was only 10.6 % in comparison to octylamine (22 %) (Yan et al., 2016). This may be attributed to the formation of a p-π conjugation effect between the nitrogen atom and the benzene ring in amino phenylboronic acid, which reduces the electron cloud density of the nitrogen atom on the acyl group. Therefore, acyl transfer to bis-amides is more challenging to occur for Mumm rearrangement, leading to a lower DS of phenylboronic acid. The FT-IR spectra of SA and SA-PBA were compared (Fig. S2d). Synthetic SA-PBA exhibits novel FT-IR signals duo to the C = O bond in the amide group (1733 cm −1) (Tang et al., 2018) and BO bond in the 3-APBA moiety (1384 cm −1) (Meng et al., 2014). The presence of amine carbonyl and BO bonds confirmed the successful grafting of 3-APBA onto the SA chain. The prepared SA-PBA was then characterized using UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. S2c). Pure SA did not exhibit any absorption peaks, however, obvious absorption peaks were observed at 260, 334, and 614 nm for SA-PBA. The absorption peaks observed at 260 and 334 nm were attributed to the π → π* absorption of the benzene ring on 3-APBA and the n → π* absorption of CO, respectively (Takahashi et al., 2018). These results prove that the hydrophobic group 3-APBA has been successfully grafted onto the hydrophilic SA chain.

Boron, an electron-deficient element, tends to accept electron-donating species to form dative bonds and Lewis pairs. Changes in the binding energy of boron and nitrogen atoms in SA-PBA were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to verify the formation of B ← N coordination bonds (Xiao et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 1a, SA-PBA comprises carbon (283.7 eV), oxygen (529.7 eV), nitrogen (398.7 eV), boron (191.6 eV), and sodium(1068.1 eV) atoms with atomic percentages of 69.06 %, 27.29 %, 2.09 %, 0.81 %, and 0.74 %, respectively. The high-resolution B 1 s and N 1 s XPS spectra of 3-APBA and SA-PBA were compared. As shown in Fig. 1b, the B 1 s signal of SA-PBA can be fitted with two peaks with binding energies of 191.6 and 189.6 eV, corresponding to (OH)-B and B ← N coordination bonds, respectively (Wu et al., 2020). The binding energy of the B 1 s of SA-PBA is lower than the binding energy of the B 1 s (191.6 eV) of 3-APBA without coordinative interactions. This indicates the enhanced electron density localized on the boron atom of SA-PBA upon forming coordinative interactions. The relative XPS results of N 1 s also confirm this transformation (Fig. 1c). As an electron donor, nitrogen in the cyclohexyl amide of the SA-PBA molecule can form B ← N coordination bonds with the electron-deficient boron atoms. By combining the above effects, SA-PBA can form stable B ← N coordination bonds in high concentrations or in the solid state (Sheepwash et al., 2011).

Having successfully synthesized amphiphilic SA-PBA, the self-assembly of SA-PBA was expected to occur in an aqueous solution. TEM images showed (Fig. 1d and e) that SA-PBA with amphiphilicity inevitably spontaneously aggregates to form regular spherical colloids in an aqueous solution. The spherical structure is formed by strong electrostatic repulsion between carboxyl groups and the strong intramolecular hydrogen-bond interactions (Dong et al., 2007). SA-PBA polymer (0.5 mg/ml) formed mainly individually dispersed soft colloidal aggregates with an average particle size of 56 nm. The soft colloids can self-assemble to form aggregates with an average size of 161 nm by intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions at a concentration of 2 mg/ml (Chenglin et al., 2012). Moreover, SA-PBA soft colloid particle size at varying concentrations was analyzed using DLS, The results showed that the particle size increased with increasing concentration, which was consistent with the trend of the TEM images (Fig. 1f). Interestingly, the diameter of the soft colloid particles measured using DLS was much larger than that observed using TEM. The size measured by the DLS is the hydrodynamic diameter, which is the diameter of an object with a strongly bound water shell generated by a continuous phase. Alternatively, this could be ascribed to the dry sample state during the TEM measurements (Wan, Zhai, et al., 2022).

Pure SA and 3-APBA are aggregation-induced blue emission substances, and it is of great interest to study the fluorescence properties of SA-PBA. Therefore, the AIE effect of the photoluminescence (PL) behavior of SA-PBA in an aqueous solution was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2a, the optimal emission wavelength and excitation wavelength of SA-PBA are 645 and 345 nm, respectively, indicating a substantial Stokes shift of 300 nm. The fluorescence spectra of SA-PBA various concentrations were measured in the range of 0.001–5 mg/ml (Fig. 2b). The results show that SA-PBA is nonemissive in dilute solutions, having an extremely low PL signal, almost parallel to the recorded abscissa. Such emission remains until the concentration increases to 0.5 mg/ml, where rather faint but visible SA-PBA emission is observed, with a maximum at ∼ 645 nm, and further enhanced emission is detected as the SA-PBA concentration increases. Notably, for concentrated solutions (i.e., 5 mg/ml), along with multiple peaks at 504, 576, and 645 nm. These multiple emissions could be ascribed to the formation of heterogeneous clusters (Ge et al., 2023). Contrary to ACQ luminogens, SA-PBA exhibits concentration-enhanced emission properties. The τf and QY of the liquid/solid-state were 2.56/3.97 ns and 1.5/14.4 %, respectively (Figs. 2c, S3). The results show that SA-PBA has a noticeable AIE effect as the concentration increases. A similar phenomenon is seen upon 365 nm UV excitation (Fig. S4). To evaluate the fluorescence intensity of SA-PBA, it was compared with 2-APBA, 3-APBA (Li et al., 2021), and SA (Dou et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 2d, the QYs of 2-APBA, 3-APBA, SA-PBA, and SA are 81.3 %, 22.7 %, 14.4 %, and 6.4 %, respectively. 2-APBA with the highest QY can self-assemble to form dimers with dynamic covalent bonds and highly ordered stacking via BN covalent and anhydride bonds. The results show that the luminous intensity of SA-PBA is between that of SA and 3-APBA, which may be because 3-APBA is an ordered structure, while SA and SA-PBA are pure organic amorphous polymers (An et al., 2021). To highlight the enhancement of fluorescence properties by the introduction of 3-APBA, SA-PBA was compared to the reported QYs of alginate-based polymers (Table S2). The results show that the introduction of 3-APBA significantly enhances the fluorescence QY of SA. These results indicate that SA-PBA is a macromolecule with AIE properties and that the addition of 3-APBA enhances its emission.

Exploration of low critical aggregation concentration (CAC) amphiphiles is of considerable importance owing to their high resistance to photobleaching and excellent photostability (Li et al., 2015). The CAC values were measured using variations in the maximum fluorescence emission intensity at different concentrations of SA-PBA in aqueous solutions. The relation between the fluorescence emission intensity of SA-PBA and the logarithm of its concentration is shown in Fig. 2e. In dilute solutions, SA-PBA does not spontaneously form colloids, and the fluorescence intensity does not change considerably. With an increase in the concentration, the fluorescence intensity corresponding to the logarithm of the concentration increases; The inflection point indicates that a large number of colloids is formed in the aqueous solution. The amphiphilic SA-PBA may form colloids with hydrophobic groups encapsulated by hydrophilic chains, and the strong hydrogen-bond interaction between the colloids restricts intramolecular motion, thus enhancing the emission of fluorophores (Yoo et al., 2008). The CAC value of SA-PBA determined using the tangent method is 0.2886 mg/ml, which is consistent with the 0.3 mg/ml solution that exhibits faint-pink emission at 365 nm UV excitation. This indicates that SA-PBA with unique AIE properties exhibits high stability in dilute solutions (Nehru et al., 2017). Optical stability is an essential parameter for luminescent materials, as associated applications usually require long irradiation periods. The change in the SA-PBA fluorescence emission intensity under various irradiated times in an aqueous solution was used to evaluate its stability. As shown in Figs. 2f and S5, the fluorescence intensity of SA-PBA was only slightly reduced after continuous irradiation with 365 nm UV light for 30 min, indicating good photostability of SA-PBA (Liu et al., 2017; Tian et al., 2017). Moreover, further study the aggregation and fluorescence behavior of SA-PBA in biological media (pH = 7.4). As shown in Fig. S6a and S6b, SA-PBA exhibit aggregation and AIE fluorescence behavior in biological media. However, the highest fluorescence intensity in the DMEM environment was weaker than that of PBS and aqueous solution at the same concentration. This may be attributed to the formation of BO ester bonds between glucose and phenylboronic acid in the DMEM medium, which disrupts the B ← N coordination bonds in the SA-PBA molecule (Chou et al., 2015). SA easily interacts with cations, and the effect of Ca2+ on the aggregation and luminescence behavior of SA-PBA was explored. As shown in Fig. S6c, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed in the SA-PBA solution without Ca2+. However, the fluorescence intensity of the SA-PBA gradually decreased with the increase of Ca2+ concentration. It is possible that the soft colloid aggregates of the SA-PBA were disrupted due to the cross-linking of SA chains and Ca2+ to form a shell-nucleus structure.

Both SA and 3-APBA are pH-responsive substances, and it is of great interest to study the changes in SA-PBA fluorescence resulting from pH changes. The fluorescence intensity of SA-PBA was measured at different pH values (i.e., 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11). As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly with increasing pH in the SA-PBA solution. This may be caused by the weakening of hydrogen bonds and, thus, the weakening of molecular interactions. When the pH value is more substantial than the pKa of 3-APBA (pKa = 8.31) (Li et al., 2017), the fluorescence emission peak shifts from 645 to 624 nm. A control group without B ← N coordination bonds was designed to demonstrate that the fluorescence emission of SA-PBA is enhanced by the formation of B ← N coordination bonds (Fig. S7a). The fluorescence spectra of SA-PBA and control groups at the same concentrations were examined in fixed testing parameters. The fluorescence intensity-concentration relationship demonstrated that SA-PBA exhibited higher fluorescence intensity than the control group at all concentrations (Fig. S7b and S7c). Notably, the fluorescence intensity of SA-PBA was fifteen times greater than that of the control group at a concentration of 5 mg/ml. This shows that the formation of an intramolecular B ← N coordination bond can effectively adjust the soft colloid microstructure and then change the position of the SA-PBA at the fluorescence emission peak. A fluorescence intensity map of 645 nm at different pH values was drawn. The histogram shows that the fluorescence intensity of SA-PBA decreases linearly with increasing pH values (Fig. 4b). A similar phenomenon is observed for SA-PBA solutions at different pH values under 365 nm UV light excitation (inset of Fig. 3b). The self-assembled morphology of SA-PBA in an aqueous solution is essential for understanding the mechanism of fluorescence intensity changes at different pH values. The morphologies of self-assembled SA-PBA colloid at different pH values were observed using TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. S8. SA-PBA can self-assemble into regular spherical colloids under acidic and neutral solution conditions, and the colloid morphology tends to be elliptical with an increase in pH values. However, colloids formed by SA-PBA in alkaline solutions are irregularly flaky and do not self-assemble well, but they tend to be associated. In an acidic environment, the carboxyl group on the SA chain is protonated, hydrophobicity, and the hydrogen bond force is strong. Simultaneously, the B ← N coordination bond is stable, which promotes the amphiphilicity of SA-PBA and the homogeneity of the colloid formed via self-assembly. By contrast, the carboxyl ionization of the SA-PBA chain and the transformation of boron atoms from hydrophobic triple coordination to a hydrophilic tetrad lead to an increase in the hydrophilicity of SA-PBA and the stretching of the molecular chain (Stubelius et al., 2019). Consequently, the molecular conformational rigidity of SA-PBA is weakened, the fluorophore formed via electron conjugation is decomposed, and the fluorescence is weakened or even quenched. Therefore, the self-assembled structure can adjust the fluorescence properties of the material, including fluorescence intensity, quantum yield, stability, and controllability, during assembly.

Notably, the fluorescence intensity of SA-PBA can be reversibly regulated because of the dynamic tunability of the hydrophilic/hydrophobic properties of boric acid (Gaballa et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 3c, SA-PBA is first depolymerized in a concentrated NaOH solution (2 M), and the fluorescence of SA-PBA decreases considerably. Interestingly, SA-PBA polymerizes again after neutralization with an HCl solution (2 M), and the fluorescence of SA-PBA returns to its original intensity. These on-off-on cycles of fluorescence intensity indicate that the depolymerization and polymerization processes of SA-PBA are reversible. The degree of polymerization of SA-PBA, namely the change in the particle size of SA-PBA duo to depolymerization and polymerization, was further measured using DLS techniques (Fig. 3d). The hydrodynamic size of SA-PBA in the initial SA-PBA solution is ~380 nm. After introducing NaOH (2 M), the hydrodynamic size increases rapidly to ~420 nm because of the depolymerization of SA-PBA. The depolymerization of SA-PBA is accompanied by a decrease in fluorescence intensity. After neutralization by the acid, the hydrodynamic size decreases to ~360 nm, suggesting the repolymerization of SA-PBA. Moreover, the repolymerization of SA-PBA after neutralization with the HCl solution leads to the recovery of the fluorescence intensity of SA-PBA. The changes in the hydrodynamic size and the corresponding fluorescence intensity indicate that the prepared SA-PBA has a polymerization-induced emission effect. These results clearly confirm that reversible polymerization of SA-PBA can be achieved by the pH-controlled reversible hydrophilic/hydrophobic properties of boric acid cleavage and reformation.

The rheological properties of amphiphilic polymer solutions are sensitive to chain association. To correlate the photophysical processes with chain interaction (aggregation), the shear viscosity modulus of the SA-PBA solutions at different concentrations was measured. As shown in Fig. 4, the concentration has a substantial effect on the shear viscosity of the solution; that is, at the same shear rate, the viscosity of the solution increases with increasing concentration. The viscosity of the SA-PBA solution remains almost constant for concentrations between 0.001 and 0.01 mg/ml, indicating Newtonian behavior. This is attributed to molecular structures being more vulnerable to damage at higher shear rates due to weak intermolecular interactions (Feng et al., 2017). At concentrations >0.1 mg/ml, however, the solution viscosity curve can be divided into two stages. In the first stage, the SA-PBA solution exhibits typical non-Newtonian fluid behavior; that is, above a specific shear rate, the viscosity monotonically decreases with increasing shear rate. Before the shear rate reaches 10 s−1, the viscosity of the solution decreases with increasing shear rate, which means that the flow is subjected to less resistance at low shear rates. This may be caused by the destruction of SA-PBA aggregates, resulting in a decrease in the viscosity of the solution with an increase in the shear rate. When the shear rate is >10 s −1, the viscosity of the solution is almost zero and remains unchanged, which may be because the SA-PBA colloid aggregates are completely destroyed, and the molecular orientation reaches its maximum (Benchabane & Bekkour, 2008). Notably, shear thinning behavior is a unique property of hydrophobic modified water-soluble polysaccharides due to hydrophobic interactions between entangled polymer chains and molecules (Leonard et al., 2004). The above results show that SA-PBA is dispersed as the chain in a dilute solution, and the intermolecular or intramolecular interaction is weak; thus, there is no fluorescence emission. However, with increasing concentrations, SA-PBA self-assembles to form colloids, and the intramolecular or intermolecular interaction is enhanced, resulting in fluorophore emission. In concentrated solutions, the intermolecular or intramolecular action of the colloid is enhanced, and localized gels form. Thus, the SA-PBA solution exhibits concentration-enhanced emission.

Molecular dynamics simulation is an efficient means of gaining insights into the microstructure of matter and active progress (Liu et al., 2023). To further reveal the luminescence mechanism of SA-PBA, the aggregation behavior of SA-PBA in an aqueous solution was analyzed using molecular dynamic simulation. The Martini force field is a coarse-grained (CG) force field applied in molecular dynamics simulations of macromolecule systems (Marrink et al., 2007). As shown in Fig. 5a and b, the CG beads of the SA-PBA structure are assigned according to the allocation of free energy between the polar and nonpolar phases of the compound. The CG of SA-PBA was defined as follows: 15 polysaccharide backbone SA (degree of substitution 20 %), with the 1st, 6th, and 11th sugar rings grafted to a hydrophobic segment on the carboxyl group (He et al., 2023). The dilute, critical, and concentrated solutions were represented by 4(8), 16, and 32 molecular chains, respectively, to simulate the aggregation behavior of SA-PBA at different concentrations. Moreover, the modeling process of SA-PBA did not strictly distinguish between the G-segment or M-segment of alginate, and two angles were defined to preserve the spatial structure of the polysaccharide backbone (Fig. 5c). In previous studies by our group, we have successfully used this model to explain experimental phenomena (Yuan et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2021). The results of the simulations, the self-aggregation-behavior simulation of SA-PBA, are summarized in Fig. 5d. In dilute solutions (Fig. 5d [i]) and [ii]), SA-PBA molecules are free to form in aqueous solution with high dispersion, which may be because SA is a strong hydrophilic polymer molecule, combines well with hydrogen bonds in water (Mazur et al., 2014). At the critical concentration (Fig. 5d [iii])), SA-PBA molecules form obvious aggregates in the aqueous solution because SA-PBA molecules form hydrophobic microregions in the solution, resulting in the formation of hydrophilic groups wrapped in hydrophobic groups (Wang et al., 2019). These include colloid aggregates with single hydrophobic microregions and combined aggregates containing multiple hydrophobic microregions. However, SA-PBA molecules in high-concentration solutions (Fig. 5d [iv]) eventually form strip-shaped association aggregates containing multiple hydrophobic microregions with larger spatial volumes, and a small number of hydrophobic groups are exposed to water (Chen et al., 2017). This may be because hydrophobic groups form larger spatial structures through hydrogen-bond interactions exhibiting stronger hydrophobicity and are likely to develop hydrophobic microregions in aqueous solutions (Yang et al., 2013). The results of CGMD show that SA-PBA can spontaneously form colloidal aggregates in high-concentration solutions and disperse in dilute solutions, which agrees with the results of the macroscopic experiments.

These results reveal the aggregation behavior of SA-PBA at different concentrations from the molecular level, including macroscopic experiments, which is helpful in further revealing the emission mechanism of SA-PBA. In dilute solutions (C < CCAC), except for possible intramolecular O···O contacts in the chain due to the failure of SA-PBA to form aggregates, the excellent dispersion and extended worm-like chain conformation result in the isolation of π and solitary (n) electrons being isolated without sufficient electronic conjugation. This indicates that interaction within the chain cannot cause the SA-BA emission. When the concentration reaches the CAC (C = CCAC), the hydrophobic interaction promotes the self-assembly of SA-PBA to form micellar structures with hydrophilic chains wrapped around hydrophobic groups and the clustering of the unconventional groups to the emission. This colloid structure also coincides with weak emission in the 0.3 mg/ml solution, indicating the clustering of unconventional groups plays a crucial role in the emission. The emission of aqueous solutions becomes progressively stronger as the concentration approaches the concentrated regime (C > CCAC). Aided by the close packing of the SA-PBA chains, hydroxyl, carboxyl, amide, and phenyl groups cluster together, resulting in the adequate overlap of n and π electrons and extended electron conjugation and simultaneous curing conformation. Meanwhile, numerous intermolecular- and intramolecular hydrogen bonds among the SA-PBA chains and partial gelation of the solution further stiffen the molecular conformation, thus giving this “cluster chromophore” enhanced emission. Therefore, it was confirmed that the fluorescence emission of SA-PBA correlated with the structure of the colloid aggregates.

Luminescent macromolecules are generally constructed by π-aromatic building blocks that function as emitting units. Recently, other types of macromolecules containing only chromophores (i.e., hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, sulfonic, urea, and amide groups) have been shown to emit fluorescence and exhibit AIE effects under appropriate conditions (Chen et al., 2018; Dong et al., 2019; Tomalia et al., 2019). In the current study, this type of macromolecule is believed to form an intermolecular cluster structure through space interactions between heteroatoms, and the formation of this clustered structure leads to the splitting of energy levels. This narrows the band gap and redshifts to the visible region and the transition of electrons between the newly generated hybrid energy levels produces visible light-emission behavior. The luminescence system of this class of compounds is also a class in the AIE system called the clustering-triggered emission (CTE) effect (Zhang, Zhao, McGonigal, et al., 2020; Zhang, Zhao, Turley, et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). The clustering of unconventional chromophores with π and lone-paired (n) electrons and the subsequent electron-cloud overlap with simultaneous conformation rigidification cause intrinsic light emission. These results show that the solution and solid states of SA-PBA can emit fluorescence, which can also be explained by the CTE effect (Fig. 6 ). The clustering of hydroxyl, carboxyl, amide, and phenyl groups in SA-PBA provides efficient through-space intermolecular and intermolecular interactions simultaneously with hydrophobic association rigid conformations. Thus, extended electron delocalization occurs, which can be excited to produce a radiation emission. Notably, SA-PBA contains a large number of hydrogen bonds that not only rigidify the molecule conformationally but also promote through-space conjugation among adjacent n and π electrons, further enhancing fluorescence emission.

To further reveal the dynamic fluorescence of SA-PBA, CGMD was used to simulate the molecular self-aggregation behavior under different pH environments. Fig. 7a shows the aggregation behavior of SA-PBA in aqueous solutions at different pH values. The snapshots presented in this figure show that SA-PBA did not form aggregates containing hydrophobic microregions under alkaline conditions. With increasing pH values, SA-PBA tends to form colloidal aggregates containing hydrophobic microns, the hydrophobic microregions of the aggregates increase, and the shape of colloid aggregates gradually changes from rod-like to spherical. This result suggests that the structural morphology of colloidal aggregates is related to the amphiphilicity of alginate-based polymers. SA-PBA self-assembles in an aqueous solution to form micellar aggregates under acidic conditions, which induces the formation of B ← N coordinate bonds and thus fluorescence emission. By contrast, SA-PBA is dispersed in an aqueous solution under alkaline conditions, the B ← N coordination bonds are dissociated and the fluorescence is attenuated or even quenched (Fig. 7b).

We then developed a simple anti-counterfeiting strategy based on pH-controlled polymerization properties for reversible information encryption (Fig. 7c). Acidic SA-PBA can be used to design code numbers because of its stable bright pink fluorescence in solid acid solutions (Fig. 7c) (Chen et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 7d, an HCl solution (2 M) was first mixed with the prepared SA-PBA (5 mg/ml). The bright pink fluorescence of SA-PBA is turned on, and the encrypted information is protected under 365 nm UV irradiation. Subsequently, after adding a NaOH solution (2 M) to make the solution environment alkaline, the pink fluorescence of SA-PBA is quenched, and a pink “2023” pattern appears. We also designed another code number for reversible information encryption (Fig. 7e). First, HCl (2 M) and NaOH (2 M) were used to adjust the SA-PBA solution to display an “888” pattern, and the HCl and NaOH solutions were then used to adjust the “888” pattern to an “HNU” pattern. Finally, the HCl and NaOH solutions were adjusted to reveal the original “888” design. Therefore, the obtained SA-PBA shows good application prospects for reversible information encryption.

In summary, we have synthesized an amphiphilic alginate polymer, SA-PBA, that exhibits the AIE characteristic of pink luminescence in both a concentrated solution and a solid state. In addition, pH can regulate the formation/depolymerization of B ← N coordination bonds to achieve dynamic fluorescence behavior by regulating the aggregation morphology of SA-PBA colloids in aqueous solutions. Moreover, the quantum yield of SA-PBA reaches 14.4 % on introducing B ← N coordination bonds, which is twice that of pure SA. The combination of CGMD simulations and experiments revealed that SA-PBA uses a hydrophobic driving force and hydrogen bond interaction to self-assemble in an aqueous solution and promote fluorescence emission. Therefore, pH-responsive SA-PBA is expected to become a new anti-counterfeited material. We believe that our approach of combining CGDM simulation and experiments can theoretically guide the luminescence mechanism of biological macromolecules.
